                 EMPLOYMENT
                 AND
                 EARNINGS
                 AND MONTHLY
                 REPORT ON                                                          Vol. 15 No. 8
                 THE LABOR FORCE                                                   February 1969
                 JOSEPH M. FINERTY, EDITOR                         JOHN E. BREGGER, ASSOCIATE EDITOR



                                                   Highlights
                           The derivation of the BLS spendable earnings series is discussed
                    by Carol Utter in The Spendable Earnings Series: A Technical Note on
                    its Calculations (pp. 6-13).

                           In accordance with regular practice at the beginning of each
                    year, the seasonally adjusted labor force series have been slightly
                    revised, due to the application of new seasonal factors which incor-
                    porate 1968 data. The revisions did not -affect the total unemployment
                    rates published in 1968 by more than 0.1 percentage point. A discussion
                    of the seasonal adjustment methodology, the new factors, and the updated
                    seasonally adjusted series appear on pages 20, 22, and 26-68, respectively.

      HOUSEHOLD DATA
      SEASONALLY ADJUSTED




                                          The seasonally adjusted series which follow cover 154 major series
                                     on the labor force 16 years of age and over. They include employment
                                     status by sex, color, and selected age groups; employment status by occu-
                                     pation; employment data by full- and part-time status; and unemployment
                                     by marital status. The March issue of Employment and Earnings and
                                     Monthly Report on the Labor Force will contain additional seasonally ad-
                                     justed series, including unemployment by duration, unemployment rates by
                                     industry, and class-of-worker s t a t i s t i c s .

                                              The majority of the seasonally adjusted labor force series presented
                                     here are computed by a method of aggregation. The process begins with the
                                     computation of seasonal factors and seasonally adjusted data for approxi-
                                     mately 100 basic labor force series. These basic series are then aggre-
                                     gated according to a set of specifications to yield other seasonally ad-
                                     justed estimates. All civilian labor force and unemployment rate s t a t i s -
                                     t i c s , as well as the major employment and unemployment estimates, are
                                     computed by this method of aggregation. Totals of one aggregation pat-
                                     tern (e.g. civilian labor force by age and sex) will not necessarily equal
                                     those of other patterns (civilian labor force by color or occupation).
                                     Since the annual averages are also a product of the aggregation proce-
                                     dures, they may differ in some instances from those published elsewhere.
                                     A l i s t of the basic series which are independently seasonally adjusted are
                                     available from the Bureau of Labor S t a t i s t i c s .

                           C-5:      Gross and spendable average weekly earnings of production or nonsupervisory workers1
                                             on private nonagricultural payrolls, in current and 1957-59 dollars

                                                      Technical Note

                                          Additional information concerning the preparation
                                          of the labor force, employment, hours and earnings,
                                          and labor turnover series—concepts and scope,
                                          survey methods, and limitations—is contained in
                                          technical notes for each of these series, available
                                          from the Bureau of Labor Statistics free of charge.



       INTRODUCTION                                                    Relation between the household and payroll series

       roll reports from employers, and (3) administrative             that the other cannot suitably supply. Population charac-

                                                    Establishment Data
       COLLECTION                                                         State and area series and then send the establishment
                                                                          data to the BLS for use in preparing the national series.
         Payroll reports provide current information on wage
      and salary employment, hours, earnings, and labor turn-
                                                                          Shuttle Schedules
      over in nonagricultural establishments, by industry and
      geographic location.                                                    Two types of data collection schedules are used:
                                                                           Form BLS 790—Monthly Report on Employment, Pay-
       Federal-State Cooperation                                          roll, and Hours; and Form DL 1219—Monthly Report
                                                                          on Labor Turnover. These schedules are of the "shuttle"
          Under cooperative arrangements with State agencies,
                                                                          type, with space for each month of the calendar year.
       the respondent fills out a single employment or labor
                                                                          The collecting agency returns the schedule to the
       turnover reporting form, which is then used for national,

                                                UNEMPLOYMENT INSURANCE DATA
         Insured unemployment represents the number of per-              claims are notices filed by those losing jobs covered by
      sons reporting a week of unemployment under an unem-               an unemployment insurance program that they are start-
      ployment insurance program. It includes some persons               ing a period of unemployment. A claimant who continues
      who are working part time who would be counted as em-              to be unemployed a full week is then counted in the
      ployed in the payroll and household surveys. Excluded              insured unemployment figure.
      are persons who have exhausted their benefit rights, new
      workers who have not earned rights to unemployment                    Because of differences in State laws and procedures
      insurance, and persons losing jobs not covered by unem-            under which unemployment insurance programs are op-
      ployment insurance systems (agriculture, State and local           erated, State unemployment rates generally indicate, but
      government, domestic service, self-employment, unpaid              do not precisely measure, differences among the indi-

                                                           ATTENTION
             As discussed in the Technical Note, the Bureau periodically adjusts the industry employ-
       ment series to a recent benchmark to improve their accuracy. These adjustments may also
       affect the hours and earnings series because employment levels are used as weights. Industry
       data for all national series shown in this report have been adjusted to March 1967 benchmarks.
       Data from April 1967 forward are subject to revision at the time of the next benchmark.

           Beginning with the June 1968 and subsequent issues of Employment and Earnings and
      Monthly Report on the Labor Force, the national data in sections B, C, and D supersede
      those published in previous issues, as well as those appearing in the Handbook of Labor Statis-
      tics, 1968. Comparable data are published in Employment and Earnings Statistics for the
      United States, 1909-68, BLS Bulletin 1312-6.
